item.title: Thirty-one families receive keys to new homes
item.description: THIRTY-ONE families from settlements between Navua and Tailevu are now homeowners after receiving the keys to their new houses at the Housing Authority’s Nepani subdivision yesterday. Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka officiated at the handover ceremony, describing the occasion as an important milestone for the families moving into their new homes.
item.content:encoded:
THIRTY-ONE families from settlements between Navua and Tailevu are now homeowners after receiving the keys to their new houses at the Housing Authority’s Nepani subdivision yesterday.

Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka officiated at the handover ceremony, describing the occasion as an important milestone for the families moving into their new homes.

“We want the children to grow up in a good home and safe parenting from father and mother,” Mr Rabuka said.

“It is a great privilege to be here today to hand over the keys to 31 Fijian families who will become proud homeowners here at Housing Authority’s Nepani subdivision.”

Mr Rabuka also revealed that Housing Minister Maciu Nalumisa visited New Zealand to study a housing initiative that repurposes leftover building materials to construct homes for vulnerable communities.

He said the minister returned convinced the model could be adapted for Fiji and that the approach had been incorporated into the new homes.

Mr Nalumisa told this newspaper the project formed part of a broader effort by Government and the Housing Authority to expand home ownership through affordable housing.

“This is part of the government as well as Housing Authority’s initiative to promote home ownership through affordable housing,” he said.

“For a long time at the national level some state housing agencies have been trying to think of strategies to promote ownership.”

Mr Nalumisa said affordability had been a key focus of the development, with the project using technology to reduce housing costs.

He said 28 of the 31 families who received homes had household incomes of less than $30,000 a year, reflecting the program’s focus on lower-income earners.

“In the long term, it’s going to benefit a lot of people in Fiji.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: NZ sugar to address shortage
item.description: SUGAR imported from New Zealand has arrived in Fiji and is already being distributed to retailers as the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) works to ease shortages that have left supermarket shelves short of supplies. In a statement, FSC said additional shipments from Thailand were expected throughout August and the coming months to supplement local production and maintain adequate supplies.
item.content:encoded:
SUGAR imported from New Zealand has arrived in Fiji and is already being distributed to retailers as the Fiji Sugar Corporation (FSC) works to ease shortages that have left supermarket shelves short of supplies.

In a statement, FSC said additional shipments from Thailand were expected throughout August and the coming months to supplement local production and maintain adequate supplies.

The corporation said the shortage was caused by an unforeseen delay to the start of the 2026 crushing season, which placed pressure on domestic sugar stocks. To manage limited supplies, FSC introduced temporary rationing to ensure sugar remained available across the country.

FSC said the situation was steadily improving, with all three mills now operating after the Lautoka Mill began crushing on July 14.

As production increases, sugar bagging has resumed and fresh stocks are being dispatched to retailers. Consumers should expect supplies to gradually return to supermarket shelves over the coming weeks.

FSC said it had made significant progress in clearing outstanding orders, with the current backlog for retail-pack sugar standing at about 65 tonnes, which it expects to clear by the end of this week. June orders for 25-kilogram bags have already been fulfilled, with deliveries now being made against current orders.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Review school grant system, says Singh
item.description: The School Management Association of Fiji is calling for an urgent review of the school grant system, warning that inadequate funding is leaving schools unable to maintain facilities and meet health and safety requirements. Association president Govind Singh made the comments after an 83-student school in Lau was given three months to address major occupational health and safety concerns or risk c
item.content:encoded:
The School Management Association of Fiji is calling for an urgent review of the school grant system, warning that inadequate funding is leaving schools unable to maintain facilities and meet health and safety requirements.

Association president Govind Singh made the comments after an 83-student school in Lau was given three months to address major occupational health and safety concerns or risk closure.

Mr Singh said existing grants were insufficient to cover repairs, particularly for rural and maritime schools.

“The grant formula in schools should be completely overhauled,” he said, questioning how urban primary schools receiving limited funding were expected to maintain standards.

He said schools faced increasing costs for repairs, ancillary staff and operations, with little or no funding available for major infrastructure work.

Mr Singh criticised the possibility of closing remote schools, saying it would unfairly affect children who already faced limited access to education.

“No school should close. It was built for a purpose. It’s still serving the purpose.”

He urged the Government to provide financial assistance for repairs and consider using agencies such as military engineers to restore ageing school facilities rather than shutting schools down.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Teacher exodus worsens
item.description: FIJI’S teacher shortage has deepened into a national crisis, with about 1000 vacancies across primary, secondary, early childhood, special and inclusive education, leaving schools struggling to replace staff and students missing valuable classroom time. Speaking at the School Management Association of Fiji’s 11th National Conference in Suva yesterday, Fiji Teachers Union general secretary Muniappa
item.content:encoded:
FIJI’S teacher shortage has deepened into a national crisis, with about 1000 vacancies across primary, secondary, early childhood, special and inclusive education, leaving schools struggling to replace staff and students missing valuable classroom time.

Speaking at the School Management Association of Fiji’s 11th National Conference in Suva yesterday, Fiji Teachers Union general secretary Muniappa Goundar said the shortage had persisted for the past three to four years as teachers resigned to join other professions or migrated to Australia and New Zealand for better opportunities.

“What they are doing is training our teachers here for export,” Mr Goundar said.

“That’s why we are having a shortage in the classroom.”

He said the crisis was compounded by about 600 secondary-trained graduates teaching in primary schools while being classified as unqualified teachers and paid less than graduate primary teachers despite performing the same duties.

“They must be paid the same graduate salary. That is one reason these teachers are going because the salary does not match the amount of work they do.”

He blamed poor salaries, heavy workloads, delayed salary payments for new graduates, limited career development and the absence of salary increases in the national budget for driving teachers out of the profession.

School Management Association of Fiji president Govind Singh said schools were waiting up to six months for replacement teachers, creating major disruptions to learning and exposing schools to occupational health and safety risks.

He said the delays were particularly damaging in primary schools, where one teacher was often responsible for an entire class.

“As soon as you see a teacher is without a class, it’s an occupational health and safety matter,” he said, urging Government to establish a reserve pool of teachers in every division to quickly fill vacancies and unexpected absences.

Ministry of Education deputy secretary Timoci Bure acknowledged the challenge, saying most schools exceeded the recommended 1:35 teacher-student ratio, with overcrowded classrooms placing further pressure on an already stretched teaching workforce.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Grower urges military deployment to save crushing season
item.description: A BA cane farmer has called on the Government to deploy Fiji Military Forces personnel to assist with manual sugarcane harvesting, saying a severe labour shortage is delaying the crushing season and contributing to concerns over local sugar supplies. Yalalevu farmer Arvind Singh said manual harvesting had stalled in many areas because of a lack of workers, despite machine harvesting continuing and
item.content:encoded:
A BA cane farmer has called on the Government to deploy Fiji Military Forces personnel to assist with manual sugarcane harvesting, saying a severe labour shortage is delaying the crushing season and contributing to concerns over local sugar supplies.

Yalalevu farmer Arvind Singh said manual harvesting had stalled in many areas because of a lack of workers, despite machine harvesting continuing and some farmers already cutting cane.

“I urge the Minister for Sugar, Tomasi Tunabuna, to please have a discussion with the Minister for Defence, Pio Tikoduadua,” Mr Singh said.

“We have so many army personnel and they can come and help in harvesting so they can make some money too rather than being idle in the army barracks.”

Mr Singh said the military had regularly supported communities following natural disasters and could similarly assist farmers facing labour shortages.

“When the flood happens, the army used to come and help in rebuilding the lives of people, whether it’s their homes, schools and other things,” he said.

“So, this is the time farmers also need them.

“I think the Minister should talk to the Defence Minister if he can arrange some army personnel to come to the sugarcane farms and help farmers. That will be a very big relief for us.”

Mr Singh stressed that farmers were not refusing to harvest their crops.

“Those who have manpower have started harvesting and the machine harvesters have started. It’s only the manual harvesting that is not operating because 90 per cent of the manpower is not there,” he said.

His comments come amid public concern over sugar shortages, with some supermarkets reporting limited supplies in recent weeks.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Mill closure disrupts plans
item.description: THE temporary closure of the Labasa Sugar Mill over the weekend disrupted harvesting plans for cane farmers waiting to deliver their crops. Vakasigani farmer Nagab Reddy said the mill suffered a breakdown on Saturday before closing on Sunday for scheduled cleaning, forcing many growers to delay deliveries.
item.content:encoded:
THE temporary closure of the Labasa Sugar Mill over the weekend disrupted harvesting plans for cane farmers waiting to deliver their crops.

Vakasigani farmer Nagab Reddy said the mill suffered a breakdown on Saturday before closing on Sunday for scheduled cleaning, forcing many growers to delay deliveries.

“We started harvesting on Saturday, but the mill broke down and we had to stop,” he said.

Mr Reddy said the disruption meant lorries already loaded with cane were unable to unload at the mill, making it difficult for other farmers to access transport.

“We couldn’t get a lorry because they were already loaded. We had to wait for the mill to start again.”

He said only farmers who already had access to lorries were able to continue transporting cane during the disruption, while others had to suspend harvesting.

Mr Reddy said the delay came at a critical stage of the crushing season when growers were trying to harvest as much cane as possible before further interruptions or poor weather.

He said any disruption to mill operations affected harvesting schedules and increased pressure on farmers, contractors and transport operators.

He hoped there would be fewer breakdowns during the remainder of the crushing season so farmers could deliver their cane without unnecessary delays.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Lease expiry a concern
item.description: THE future remains uncertain for some cane farmers in Waiqele, Labasa, as their agricultural leases edge closer to expiry. Among them is Naleen Devi, who said she had only five years remaining on her lease and was worried about what would happen when it ended.
item.content:encoded:
THE future remains uncertain for some cane farmers in Waiqele, Labasa, as their agricultural leases edge closer to expiry.

Among them is Naleen Devi, who said she had only five years remaining on her lease and was worried about what would happen when it ended.

“My lease is about to expire. I only have about five years left,” she said.

“I don’t know what will happen. We do not have money and we do not have a proper house.”

Ms Devi said her family had relied on cane farming for many years, but increasing production costs and labour shortages had made it difficult to improve their living conditions.

“We only have a temporary home because all our money goes into farming.”

She said farmers continued to battle rising costs for fertiliser, labour and other farm inputs while receiving limited returns from their crops.

The uncertainty surrounding lease renewals had added another burden for growers who depended on the land to support their families.

Ms Devi said many farmers had invested years of hard work into developing their farms and hoped their leases would be renewed. Without secure access to land, she feared many growers would struggle to continue farming and earn a living, placing additional pressure on families already facing financial hardship.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Experts: Public service weakened
item.description: YEARS of political upheaval have left Fiji’s public service weakened, risk-averse and still grappling with the legacy of successive coups, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission has heard. Academics and governance experts told the Commission political instability had reshaped government institutions, eroded public confidence and created a culture in which public servants were increasingly relucta
item.content:encoded:
YEARS of political upheaval have left Fiji’s public service weakened, risk-averse and still grappling with the legacy of successive coups, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission has heard.

Academics and governance experts told the Commission political instability had reshaped government institutions, eroded public confidence and created a culture in which public servants were increasingly reluctant to make decisions without higher approval.

University of the South Pacific professor Vijay Naidu said successive coups had undermined social cohesion and weakened the rule of law, leaving lasting effects across the public service.

He said government operations had slowed as public servants became more cautious, with decision-making and procurement processes increasingly delayed because officials feared making mistakes or acting without authorisation.

USP governance professor Graham Hassall said the transformation of the public service was driven by about 600 decrees, promulgations, regulations and orders introduced between the 1987 and 2013 coup periods. He identified the 2009 State Services Decree as one of the most significant reforms, saying it reshaped public administration by removing senior office holders and changing appointment processes across government.

Governance specialist Emele Duituturaga told the Commission the 2006 coup had profound personal consequences for many public servants, with senior officials losing their positions without any avenue to challenge the decisions.

“The public service is a human endeavour,” she said.

“When politics takes over, it doesn’t just disrupt services, it takes over people’s lives.”

Emele Duituturaga while giving her ideas during the Fiji Truth and Reconciliation Commission expert roundtable discussion at the Suva Civic Centre lower hall augitorium on Tuesday, July 21, 2026. Picture: JONACANI LALAKOBAU

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
